sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Rice is one of the world’s most important cereal crops and a staple food for more than half of the global population. It belongs to the Poaceae family (the grass family). Rice farming is traditionally practiced in regions with high rainfall or where controlled irrigation is available. Key Characteristics of Rice: Grows best in standing water (paddy conditions). Plant height varies between 80 to 150 cm, depending on variety. Has a slender stem, long leaves, and a grain-bearing panicle. Its root system is fibrous, enabling it to survive in partially flooded fields. Global Importance: Rice production exceeds 700 million tons globally. Main producers: China, India, Bangladesh, Vietnam, Thailand, Pakistan. It is a major traded commodity and essential for food security. Why Rice Is Important for Farmers: High demand in both local and export markets. Stable income source. Suitable for areas with abundant water. By-products (bran, husk, straw) are also valuable.

sections.heading: Climate Requirement
sections.content: Rice is a tropical and subtropical crop that needs warm, moist conditions. Temperature: Germination: 16 to 40°C (optimum 25 to 35°C) Tillering stage: 25 to 30°C Reproductive stage: 20 to 25°C Grain filling: 20 to 30°C Cold sensitivity: Below 18°C slows growth Rainfall & Humidity: Requires 1,000 to 2,000 mm of rainfall annually. Best humidity: 60 to 80% High humidity helps in photosynthesis and grain filling. Sunlight: Rice requires 4 to 5 hours of direct sunlight daily. Cloudy weather during flowering can reduce grain formation.

sections.heading: Soil Requirement
sections.content: Rice grows best in soils that can retain water. Best Soil Types: Clay soil: excellent for water retention Clay loam: good fertility and moisture holding Silty loam: supports strong vegetative growth Soil pH: Ideal pH: 5.5 to 7.0 Slightly acidic soils are preferred. Important Soil Properties: Soil should be deep for strong roots. Should contain high organic matter. Good water retention is crucial for paddy conditions. Soil Preparation: First plowing to break clods Flooding of field Puddling (mixing soil and water to make a soft, level base) Levelling to ensure uniform water depth

sections.heading: Sowing Time
sections.content: Rice can be grown through: A. Nursery (Transplanting Method): Most common method in Pakistan and Asia. Sowing Time (Kharif Season): Punjab: May to June Sindh: June to July Nursery Duration: Seedlings are raised for 25 to 30 days, then transplanted. B. Direct Seeding: Seeds are sown directly into the field (either dry or wet seeding). Advantages: Saves labor Faster sowing Seed Rate: Transplanting: 5 to 8 kg/acre Direct seeding: 12 to 15 kg/acre

sections.heading: Fertilizer Schedule
sections.content: Rice needs N (Nitrogen), P (Phosphorus), K (Potassium) in large amounts. Recommended Dose (General): Nitrogen (Urea): 100 to 150 kg/acre Phosphorus (DAP): 20 to 30 kg/acre Potassium (SOP or MOP): 20 to 25 kg/acre Zinc Sulfate: 10 to 12 kg/acre (essential for tillering) Application Schedule: 1. Basal Dose (before transplanting): DAP + SOP Half dose of urea 2. Tillering Stage (25 to 30 days after transplanting): 25% urea 3. Panicle Initiation Stage (45 to 55 days after transplanting): Remaining 25% urea Why Fertilizer is Important? Nitrogen increases tillers (more branches) Phosphorus helps strong roots Potassium improves grain filling and disease resistance

sections.heading: Weeds, Pests and Diseases
sections.content: A. Common Weeds: Cyperus rotundus (Dela) Water grass Barnyard grass Weed Control: Pre-emergence herbicides (e.g., Butachlor) Manual weeding Maintaining water level to suppress weeds B. Major Pests: Stem Borer: Whitish caterpillar that bores into stems. Damage: dead hearts, whiteheads Leaf Folder: Rolls leaves and feeds inside Brown Plant Hopper (BPH): Sucks sap, causes 'hopper burn' Pest Control: Spray Carbofuran, Imidacloprid, or Fipronil depending on pest Maintain water drainage to control BPH C. Major Diseases: Blast Disease Sheath Blight Bacterial Leaf Blight (BLB) Disease Control: Use resistant varieties Proper drainage Fungicides: Tricyclazole, Propiconazole Avoid excessive nitrogen

sections.heading: Harvesting
sections.content: When to Harvest: Rice is ready for harvest when: Grains become golden yellow Moisture content is 20 to 24% 80 to 90% grains are mature

sections.heading: Check out the videos below to learn about Rice farming

urdu.title: چاول کی فصل کی مکمل معلومات

sections.heading: تعارف
sections.content:
چاول دنیا کی اہم ترین اناج فصلوں میں سے ایک ہے اور آدھی سے زیادہ عالمی آبادی کی بنیادی غذا ہے۔ یہ گھاس کے خاندان سے تعلق رکھتا ہے۔ چاول کی کاشت زیادہ بارش یا آبپاشی والے علاقوں میں کی جاتی ہے۔

اہم خصوصیات
پانی میں بہتر اگتا ہے
پودے کی اونچائی 80 سے 150 سینٹی میٹر
باریک تنا اور لمبے پتے
ریشے دار جڑیں

اہمیت
700 ملین ٹن سے زیادہ پیداوار
بڑے ممالک چین، بھارت، بنگلہ دیش، پاکستان

کسانوں کے لیے فائدہ:
زیادہ طلب
مستحکم آمدنی
ضمنی مصنوعات بھی قیمتی ہیں

sections.heading: موسمی ضروریات
sections.content:
چاول ایک گرم اور نیم گرم موسمی فصل ہے جو گرم اور مرطوب ماحول چاہتی ہے۔
درجہ حرارت
اگنا: 
نمی 60 سے 80 فیصد

sections.heading: مٹی کی ضروریات
sections.content:
چاول ایسی مٹی میں اگتا ہے جو پانی روک سکے۔
چکنی، میرا، اور سلٹی مٹی بہترین ہے
pH 5.5 سے 7

sections.heading: بوائی کا وقت
sections.content:
پنجاب میں مئی سے جون
سندھ میں جون سے جولائی
نرسری طریقہ عام ہے

sections.heading: کھاد
sections.content:
نائٹروجن، فاسفورس اور پوٹاشیم ضروری ہیں
یوریا، ڈی اے پی، ایس او پی استعمال ہوتے ہیں

sections.heading: کیڑے اور بیماریاں
sections.content:
اہم کیڑے: سٹیم بورر، لیف فولڈر
اہم بیماریاں: بلاسٹ، بیکٹیریل لیف بلائٹ

sections.heading: کٹائی
sections.content: جب دانے سنہری ہو جائیں اور نمی 20 سے 24 فیصد ہو تو فصل تیار ہوتی ہے
